schools.summary: Bede: 'by what authority' is a denial, hinting his works are of the devil — most plainly denying the Son of God; Theophylact/Eusebius: he will answer by John, exposing their rebellion against the Spirit.
The last temple-teaching day (Tuesday); the rulers 'stood up against him' (epestesan); the whole Sanhedrin confronts his authority (Matt 21:23; Mark 11:27).
Henry: Christ a preacher of his own gospel, publishing the salvation he purchased.
A double question — the kind of authority and its source; Luke's peremptory 'tell us' (eipon hemin); 'these things' embracing the temple-cleansing, teaching, and works — a trap framed as inquiry.
He answers the trap not with a direct claim but 'one word' (logon, Mark 11:29; Matt 21:24) — a counter-question, requiring them to commit themselves first.
Gill: John's baptism, a new ordinance, must derive from God or men; by the same authority John baptized, Christ preached — John's authority and Christ's stand or fall together.
They 'reasoned with themselves' (synelogisanto, only here in NT) — weighing not what is true but what is safe; 'From heaven' would convict them of unbelief in John and in the One he proclaimed.
Luke's vivid 'all the people will stone us' (katalithasei, only here); the crowd's settled persuasion that John was a prophet (Matt 14:5); the rulers governed by fear of the people, not truth.
Gill: they told an untruth, sacrificing conscience to interest, pretending ignorance rather than confessing the truth they knew.
Robertson: the indirect assertion 'that they knew not' (me eidenai) — a feigned ignorance.
Gill: since they would not answer his question, he declines to answer theirs — a just refusal, truth not owed to those who suppress the truth they know.
Ambrose: the vineyard is the house of Israel (Isa 5:1), planted by God; Bede: the planter the same who hired labourers; the parable foretells what they would do to Christ.

Eusebius: a parable foretelling their crime; Cyril: the 'far country' God withdrawing his visible presence after Sinai, yet still sending prophets; Chrysostom: Christ came after the prophets by divine design.
Henry: by questioning his authority they forfeited their own — disowning the vineyard's lord voided their lease.
'At the season' the definite fruit-time (Matt 21:34); the servant beaten and sent empty (almost verbal agreement with Mark) — the first prophet met with violence.
Theophylact: the 'fruit' God seeks is not gain for himself but the people's obedience and knowledge of him, which is their profit.
The Hebraism 'he added to send another' (prosetheto pempsai); Luke's emphatic repeated 'sent him away empty' — the pattern of rejection deepening, a later set of prophets abused.
'They wounded him' (traumatisantes) peculiar to Luke, with a half-surgical ring (cf Acts 19:16) — the abuse escalating from insult to injury, the casting-out anticipating the Son's fate.
'What shall I do?' and 'it may be' (isos, only here) are Luke's peculiar touches; the owner's last resort is his 'beloved son' — Christ's self-disclosure as the Son sent above the servants.

themes: 'it may be they will reverence him'

schools.summary: They know him as 'the heir' (Ps 2:8; Heb 1:2) and for that reason plot his death (Acts 2:23) to seize the inheritance — the crime deliberate, not ignorant; covetous refusal to render God his due.

Gill: the Son cast out of the vineyard and slain answers to Christ rejected as Messiah, delivered to the Gentiles, and crucified outside the city; the question 'what shall the lord do?' draws the hearers to judge their own case.

Luke (with Mark) puts the sentence in Christ's lips, not the bystanders'; 'God forbid' = me genoito (no 'God' in the Greek), the protest of the defeated Sanhedrin seeing the parable turn against them; the destruction fulfilled at Jerusalem's fall (Neh 9:36).
Wesley: they allowed such wretches deserved destruction but could not bear it applied to themselves.

'He beheld them' (emblepsas) a piercing glance (not in Mark/Matthew); the Scripture is Ps 118:22 — the builders (Israel's leaders) reject the stone (Christ), whom God makes the head of the corner, sealing the parable with Scripture.

'Broken' (synthlasthesetai, shatter) and 'grind to powder' (likmesei, winnow) — found in all MSS of Luke (disputed in Matt 21:44); the stone of Dan 2:34; Gill distinguishes stumbling at Christ in unbelief from being crushed in judgment.

themes: the stone falls, ground to powder

schools.summary: Bede: by seeking to slay him they prove the parable true, being the wicked husbandmen; Cyril: warned, they turned not from evil but gathered fresh occasion for crime.
They sought 'in that very hour' to lay hands on him, checked only by fear of the people — understanding the parable, they answered with murderous intent, not repentance.
'Watched him' (paratoresantes) insidiously; 'spies' (enkathetous) set in ambush, feigning righteousness to catch his words and deliver him to the governor as a political offender.
Henry: the plot was to deliver him to the governor's power — to make him a seditious offender.
The spies (Pharisees' disciples with Herodians, Matt 22:16; Mark 12:13) open with flattery, endorsing his impartial teaching ('acceptest not the person,' cf Gal 2:6) to provoke an unguarded answer.

'Tribute' (phoron, the land-tax; Mark/Matthew's Latin kenson); the emphatic 'for us' frames it as whether God's people may pay a heathen ruler — a dilemma where 'pay' offends the crowd and 'do not pay' invites a charge of sedition.

'Perceived' (katanoesas, mind set down on it) their 'craftiness' (panourgian; Matthew 'wickedness,' Mark 'hypocrisy,' cf Paul's use, 2 Cor 4:2) — the discerner of hearts sees through the disguise before answering.

The tribute-coin a Roman denarius bearing Caesar's image and inscription; by making them answer 'Caesar's,' the Lord leads them to supply the premise — to use Caesar's coin is to acknowledge his authority.
Render Caesar his due — lawful taxes (Rom 13:7) — and God his; the two spheres distinguished, neither denied, escaping sedition and impiety alike.
Origen: as the coin bears Caesar's image, so man bears God's image — the soul stamped with God's image must be rendered to God.
The spies make an ignominious failure — no case for surrendering Jesus to Pilate; they marvel and fall silent, the trap baited with flattery catching nothing; Luke fuller on both plot and defeat.

themes: could not take hold of his words

schools.summary: Bede: the Sadducees claimed a righteousness not theirs; Origen: their heresy denies the resurrection and holds the soul dies with the body; they came watching to entrap him.
After the rout of Pharisees and Herodians, the Sadducees rally with their stock conundrum (Matt 22:23; Acts 23:8) — the materialist party that denies the resurrection.

They cite the levirate law (Deut 25:5) — a brother raising up seed to one who died childless — grounding their riddle in the Pentateuch the Sadducees fully owned, as scaffolding for a reductio against the resurrection.

Seven brothers in succession take one wife under the levirate law, the first dying childless — a supposed (likely invented) case framed to press the law to an absurdity against the resurrection (cf Matt 22:23).
Gill: the second brother took her to wife as obliged by the levirate law — the deliberate repetition fixing the multiplied claim on one woman, assuming this life's bonds carry into the next.

Gill: the third and all seven took her by the same law — the case completed, one woman wife to seven, the deliberate fullness sharpening the coming question, all resting on the assumption that marriage persists beyond the grave.
Gill: last of all the woman died, childless by all seven — the case completed on the far side of death, the neat construction betraying its purpose as contrived ridicule, not honest inquiry.

'Had her' (eschon, constative aorist including all seven, Matt 22:28); the mocking question 'whose wife?' rests on the false assumption that the risen life prolongs this one's marriages — the error the Lord will overturn.

The contrast of 'this world/age' and 'that world' is peculiar to Luke (cf 16:8); marriage belongs to this world of mortality, where procreation repairs the waste of death — striking at the riddle's false premise.

'Accounted worthy' a Luke/Paul word (2 Thess 1:5); those who obtain that world neither marry nor are given in marriage — the institution of a dying age gone; Gill: worthiness through Christ's righteousness, not merit.

The risen 'cannot die any more,' so are 'equal unto the angels' (isangeloi, only here) and 'sons of the resurrection'; death and marriage correlative — remove death, marriage's purpose ceases (cf 1 Cor 15:42; Rom 8:17).
Christ proves the resurrection from Moses (Exod 3:6) — the Sadducees' own authority — 'shewed' = pointed to; God calls himself the God of the long-dead patriarchs.
Calvin: the order — first repel the objection, then confirm the doctrine from Scripture, that adversaries know they oppose God's word.

God is not God of the dead but the living; Luke alone adds 'all live unto him' — the covenant relation guarantees the patriarchs live to God and shall rise; 'God of' implies no relation with the extinct (Wesley).
Calvin: believers, after death, lead a heavenly life with God (Rom 14:8) — the dead in Christ alive in his presence.

The scribes (Pharisees, who held the resurrection) praise his reply against their Sadducee rivals — 'thou hast well said' — hostile to him yet pleased; from the better section who welcomed the defence.
Calvin: the confession extorted by a secret exercise of divine power from men ill-disposed toward him.
None of any party — Pharisees, Sadducees, Herodians — dared ask more, for fear of fresh confounding; Luke omits the great-commandment question; the questioners reduced to total silence.

Theophylact: near his Passion he proclaims his Godhead modestly, by a question leaving them to reason it out; Cyril: how is David's son David's Lord, but by divine lordship? (against dividing the one Christ into two).
'They say' = the scribes (Matt 22:42; Mark 12:35); the Lord, now on the offensive, presses beyond the Christ's human descent.

themes: 'how say they Christ is David's son?'

schools.summary: David is treated as author of Ps 110:1, its Messianic character affirmed in all three Synoptics (Acts 2:34); the Talmud once applied it to Messiah; 'no man could answer.'
Cyril of Jerusalem: the Lord says this to the Lord — not a servant but the Lord of all and his own Son.
Clarke: 'the footstool of thy feet' — the enemies, including the rulers now plotting his death, shall be completely subjected under the Christ they despise.
Cyril of Jerusalem joins it to the Son's session at the right hand of power — the Messiah's total conquest of his foes.

If David calls the Christ 'Lord,' how is he David's son? The deity and humanity of the Messiah both set forth in Ps 110:1 — reconciled only in one truly man (David's son) and truly God (David's Lord).
Chrysostom: having silenced them by the Prophets, he warns against them; Cyril: their passions were vainglory and greed, which he warns the disciples to avoid.
'In the hearing of all the people' (genitive absolute) — the right moment to speak, the warning for the disciples but the exposure for all (Mark 12:38).
The catalogue of scribal vanity — long robes, marketplace greetings, chief seats and rooms (Matt 23:5; Luke 11:43); a religion turned into a parade of self-importance.
Cyril/Theophylact: the root is the love of vainglory and the hunt for reputation, honour, and gain.
Gill: their avarice — voracious and cruel, devouring widows' substance, not sparing the defenceless.
The gravest charges — devouring widows' houses (Matt 23:14), long prayers for a 'shew' (1 Thess 2:5), and 'greater damnation' (Jas 3:1) for sin cloaked in sanctity.
